course_info.title: Data-Driven Design
prerequisites: UX406 - UX Strategy and Business Impact

learning_objectives: Integrate analytics data into the UX design process
Distinguish between descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics
Identify which user behaviors can and cannot be measured through analytics
readings: Lean Analytics by Alistair Croll, Ch. 1-3
Designing with Data by Rochelle King, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Data-driven design uses quantitative evidence from analytics, experiments, and behavioral data to inform and validate design decisions. It complements qualitative research by revealing patterns across thousands of users.
key_concepts: Analytics hierarchy: descriptive (what happened), diagnostic (why), predictive (what will happen), prescriptive (what to do)
Behavioral analytics: clicks, scrolls, sessions, funnels, heatmaps
The quantitative-qualitative balance: analytics shows what users do, research shows why
Instrumentation planning: deciding what to measure before building
Data ethics: privacy, consent, and responsible data use
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a real Google Analytics dashboard for an e-commerce site — identifying drop-off points in the purchase funnel and forming hypotheses for usability problems

review.value: Descriptive: what happened (bounce rate is 65%). Diagnostic: why (exit page analysis shows pricing page). Predictive: what will happen (conversion will drop 3% with this change). Prescriptive: what to do (redesign the pricing page).
Analytics tells you WHAT users do (65% drop off at checkout). Qualitative research tells you WHY (users were confused by the shipping cost appearing late). Both are needed for good design decisions.

learning_objectives: Build and analyze conversion funnels to identify drop-off points
Use heatmaps and session recordings to understand page-level behavior
Segment users to uncover behavior differences between groups
readings: Web Analytics 2.0 by Avinash Kaushik, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Behavioral analytics reveals how users actually interact with your product at scale. Funnels show where users drop off, heatmaps show where they click and scroll, and session recordings show individual journeys.
key_concepts: Conversion funnels: defining steps, measuring drop-off rates, identifying bottlenecks
Heatmap types: click maps, scroll maps, move maps, attention maps
Session recordings: watching individual user journeys for pattern identification
User segmentation: new vs. returning, mobile vs. desktop, by traffic source
Cohort analysis: tracking behavior changes over time for user groups
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a checkout funnel in Mixpanel, identifying the highest drop-off step, then examining heatmaps and session recordings of that step to form design hypotheses

review.value: Define the steps users must complete to reach a goal, measure the percentage that completes each step, and focus design effort on the step with the largest drop-off — the biggest opportunity for improvement.
Show how far down a page users scroll — typically revealing that only 20-30% reach the bottom. Critical CTAs or content placed below the fold may be invisible to most users.

learning_objectives: Design rigorous A/B experiments with valid hypotheses and appropriate controls
Calculate required sample sizes and test duration
Interpret results while avoiding common statistical pitfalls
readings: Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments by Kohavi, Tang & Xu, Ch. 3-7
Evan Miller: How Not to Run an A/B Test

lecture_notes.introduction: A/B testing is the gold standard for validating design decisions with statistical rigor. It eliminates opinion-based debates by letting user behavior determine which design performs better.
key_concepts: Experiment design: hypothesis, control, variant, primary metric, guardrail metrics
Sample size calculation: minimum detectable effect, significance level, power
Avoiding pitfalls: peeking, multiple testing correction, novelty effects
Multivariate testing: when to test multiple variables simultaneously
Bayesian vs. frequentist approaches to experiment analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete A/B test for a pricing page redesign — forming the hypothesis, calculating sample size, setting guardrail metrics, and interpreting simulated results
setup_requirements: A/B test sample size calculator (Evan Miller's)

review.value: Metrics that must NOT get worse when optimizing the primary metric. If optimizing signup rate, guard against: increased churn (people signed up but left), decreased engagement, increased support tickets.
The smallest improvement you care about detecting. A 0.1% conversion lift may be statistically real but not worth the engineering effort. Define what improvement justifies the change.

learning_objectives: Design personalized experiences using behavioral data and user preferences
Balance personalization benefits with filter bubble and privacy concerns
Implement progressive personalization that improves over time
readings: Designing with Data by Rochelle King, Ch. 5-7
The Filter Bubble by Eli Pariser, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Personalization uses data to tailor the experience to individual users. When done well, it reduces noise and increases relevance. When done poorly, it feels creepy, creates filter bubbles, or makes wrong assumptions.
key_concepts: Personalization types: content-based, collaborative filtering, contextual, explicit preferences
Progressive personalization: starting generic, improving with use
The cold start problem: personalizing with no user data yet
Filter bubbles and echo chambers: ethical concerns with over-personalization
User control: always letting users override or disable personalization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a personalized news feed experience — balancing relevance with discovery, giving users visibility into why content was selected, and providing controls to adjust

review.value: New users have no behavioral data for personalization. Solutions: explicit onboarding preferences ('Select topics you like'), popular/trending content as default, demographic-based initial recommendations.

Users should always be able to see WHY something was personalized for them and have the ability to adjust. 'Recommended because you watched X' builds trust. Invisible personalization feels manipulative.

learning_objectives: Create data visualizations that effectively communicate UX findings to stakeholders
Select appropriate chart types for different data stories
Build UX dashboards that enable ongoing monitoring of design impact
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Ch. 4-8
Information Dashboard Design by Stephen Few, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Data becomes actionable only when it is clearly communicated. Data visualization transforms raw numbers into stories that stakeholders understand, believe, and act upon.
key_concepts: Chart selection: bar charts for comparison, line charts for trends, funnels for conversion
Dashboard design: context, hierarchy, and actionability
Avoiding chart junk: unnecessary decoration that obscures data
Annotation and narrative: guiding interpretation with context
Real-time vs. periodic dashboards: monitoring vs. reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a UX impact dashboard showing conversion funnel, A/B test results, SUS trends, and task success rates — with annotations guiding stakeholder interpretation
setup_requirements: Data visualization tool (Google Sheets, Figma, or Tableau)

review.value: Comparison: bar chart. Trend over time: line chart. Part-to-whole: pie chart (max 5 slices) or stacked bar. Distribution: histogram. Correlation: scatter plot. Always choose clarity over novelty.
Every metric on a dashboard should answer the question 'What would I do differently if this number changed?' If nothing would change, the metric does not belong on the dashboard.

questions.question: An e-commerce checkout funnel shows: Cart (100%) > Shipping (72%) > Payment (58%) > Confirmation (51%). Where should the design team focus, and what analytics would you examine next?
questions.answer: Focus on the Cart-to-Shipping step which has the largest absolute drop-off (28%). Next analytics to examine: (1) Segment the drop-off by device — if mobile drop-off is 40% vs. desktop 15%, the problem is likely mobile-specific (form usability, keyboard issues). (2) Check heatmaps on the cart page — where are users clicking? Are they looking for a promo code field, confused by shipping cost estimates, or unable to find the checkout button? (3) Review session recordings of users who abandoned at this step — look for patterns like hesitation, rage clicks, or scrolling confusion. (4) Check if the drop-off correlates with specific cart values (high-value carts may have more abandonment due to price anxiety).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Cart-to-Shipping as the focus area
Proposes relevant next analytics to examine
Suggests segmentation to narrow the problem

questions.question: A scroll map shows that only 15% of users scroll past 50% of a long product page. The 'Add to Cart' button is at 75% page depth. What design changes would you recommend?
questions.answer: Recommendations: (1) Move the 'Add to Cart' button above the fold or to a sticky position that follows the user as they scroll — 85% of users never see the current placement. (2) Restructure the page to put the most important conversion elements (price, key features, reviews summary, and CTA) in the top 30% where most users see them. (3) Place detailed specifications, full reviews, and secondary content below — users who need this information will scroll for it. (4) Consider adding a floating 'Add to Cart' bar that appears when the user scrolls past the primary CTA. The core principle: critical actions should be visible to 100% of users, not just the 15% who scroll the entire page.

rubric.key: Recommends moving CTA above the fold or sticky
Suggests content restructuring based on scroll data
Proposes specific solutions backed by the data

questions.question: A team wants to A/B test five different hero images on their homepage simultaneously. What are the problems with this approach, and what would you recommend instead?
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Testing 5 variants requires a much larger sample size — traffic is split 5 ways, so each variant gets only 20% of the traffic, dramatically increasing the time needed to reach statistical significance. (2) Multiple comparison problem — with 5 variants, the probability of a false positive finding increases. Without correction (like Bonferroni), a 5% significance level effectively becomes ~19%. (3) The more variants, the harder to isolate why one won — was it the subject, color, composition, or text? Recommendation: (1) First, narrow to 2-3 candidates through qualitative testing (preference test or stakeholder review). (2) Test 2 variants at a time (A/B, not A/B/C/D/E) with a proper sample size calculation. (3) If you must test 5, use a multivariate testing framework with multiple comparison correction.

rubric.key: Identifies sample size and traffic splitting problem
Identifies multiple comparison / false positive risk

questions.question: Explain the cold start problem in personalization and describe three strategies to address it for a new user on a music streaming app.
questions.answer: The cold start problem: new users have no behavioral data, so the system cannot make personalized recommendations. Without solving this, new users see generic content that may not match their taste, reducing the chance they stay. Three strategies for a music streaming app: (1) Explicit onboarding — ask users to select 5+ favorite artists or genres during signup. Quick, gives immediate signal, but relies on user effort. (2) Popular/trending content — show top charts and trending playlists as default recommendations. Not personalized but familiar and safe. (3) Contextual signals — use time of day, device type, and demographic data from signup for initial recommendations (workout playlists at 6am, chill playlists at 10pm). Progressive personalization then improves as the user listens.

rubric.key: Correctly explains the cold start problem
Strategies are appropriate for music streaming context

major_assignments.title: Data-Driven Redesign
major_assignments.description: Analyze analytics data for a real website, identify the highest-impact UX improvement opportunity, design and A/B test a solution, and measure the results. Deliver a complete data-driven design case study.
objectives: Analyze behavioral analytics to identify UX improvement opportunities
Form data-backed hypotheses for design changes
Design an A/B test and measure results
Present findings as a data-driven design case study
deliverables: Analytics analysis report: funnel analysis, heatmaps, segmentation findings
Design hypothesis document: what you will change, why, and predicted impact
A/B test design: hypothesis, variants, sample size, metrics, duration

criteria: Funnel analysis identifies specific drop-off points
Heatmaps and recordings corroborate quantitative findings
Segmentation reveals user group differences
Findings are prioritized by impact potential
Hypothesis is specific, measurable, and falsifiable
Design solution directly addresses the identified problem
A/B test is properly designed with sample size calculation
Guardrail metrics defined
Redesigned variant is well-crafted and implements the hypothesis
Test results are properly analyzed with statistical rigor
Interpretation avoids common pitfalls (peeking, multiple testing)
Conclusions match the evidence
Case study tells a clear narrative from data to design to results
Visualizations effectively communicate findings
Business impact is quantified where possible
Recommendations are actionable
Thorough analytics analysis identifying real opportunities
Well-formed hypothesis with proper A/B test design
Quality redesign and rigorous results analysis
Compelling case study presentation with business impact

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on analytics, experimentation, and data interpretation

analytics_labs.description: Weekly labs analyzing real analytics data and designing experiments

data_driven_redesign.description: Complete data-driven redesign project with A/B testing

dashboard_project.description: UX metrics dashboard design for ongoing product monitoring

final_presentation.description: Case study presentation of data-driven redesign with measured results

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve hands-on analytics tool work. Attendance expected. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Analytics phases are sequential. Late submissions penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Analytics tools may be shared. All analysis, hypotheses, and designs must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Analytics data must come from real sources. Fabricating data or test results is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Google Analytics (demo account), Hotjar or FullStory (free tier), A/B test sample size calculator, spreadsheet software, and data visualization tool.
